title: Home Workout Equipment: What's Actually Worth Buying First
excerpt: You don't need a full home gym to get started. Here's the order most people should actually buy equipment in.
author: StoreForLess Team
body:
**Resistance bands before dumbbells**

Bands are cheaper, take up almost no storage space, and cover a similar range of resistance as a small dumbbell set for most bodyweight-assisted exercises. They're the lowest-cost way to test whether a home workout habit sticks before investing more.

**A yoga mat is not optional, even for weight training**

Beyond yoga, a mat protects floors, cushions joints during floor exercises, and gives consistent footing for standing exercises. It's one of the cheapest pieces of equipment with the most daily use.

**Adjustable dumbbells beat a fixed set for small spaces**

A single adjustable dumbbell pair replacing 5-6 fixed pairs saves significant space and money over time, even though the upfront cost is higher than one fixed pair.

**Skip the fancy tracker at first**

Fitness trackers are useful for people with an established routine who want data. For someone just starting out, the habit matters more than the metrics — add tracking once the routine is consistent for a month or two.

**Storage matters more than people plan for**

Equipment that's hard to store gets used less. Foldable or stackable options (resistance bands, foldable mats, stackable weight plates) tend to get used more consistently than bulky alternatives.
